sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equity markets posted their strongest month of 2026 in May, defying an elevated geopolitical risk environment centred on the ongoing US-Iran conflict to deliver broad-based gains across Developed and Emerging Markets alike. The MSCI World extended its recovery from the March selloff, propelled almost exclusively by a historic re-rating in AI-linked megacap technology and semiconductor names; the S&P 500 gained 5.3% to set a new closing record at 7,599.96, its ninth consecutive weekly advance -- a streak observed only four times in the past four decades. Developed Market ex-US indices also participated, with Japanese equities delivering 6.2% as AI-driven chip demand eclipsed macro headwinds, while European bourses clawed back mid-month geopolitical losses to finish modestly higher. Emerging Markets outperformed materially, up approximately 9.7%, underpinned by continued AI infrastructure buildout and a softening dollar. The month's defining contradiction was that record stock prices coexisted with oil remaining north of $100/bbl -- a testament to how powerfully earnings momentum and the AI secular theme can override conventional macro-inflation headwinds when corporate fundamentals are sufficiently robust.

sections.title: Your Must-Know Market Themes
sections.content: Four key themes that shaped markets this month:
themes: AI Mega-Cycle Overpowers Geopolitical Headwinds: The singular force animating global risk appetite in May was the relentless demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure. The S&P 500 technology sector surged approximately 20% for the month alone -- the strongest sectoral return in decades -- as first-quarter 2026 earnings came in roughly 27% above prior-year levels, more than double consensus expectations, with hyperscalers and GPU manufacturers reporting record order backlogs. The Nasdaq Composite gained 8.9% and the Nasdaq 100 gained 10.6%, with NVIDIA, Broadcom, and peer semiconductor names accounting for a disproportionate share of index-level returns. Critically, this rally was not a valuation-expansion story alone; it was validated by hard earnings beats across the AI supply chain, cementing investor conviction that the secular AI build-out is at an early-to-mid stage with no near-term demand saturation. US stocks posted their best May performance since 1990, underscoring the historic magnitude of this risk appetite surge.

US-Iran Conflict -- Oil Shock Peaks, Peace Signals Emerge: The residual tail risk from the February 28 US-Israel military operation against Iran continued to exert upward pressure on energy prices throughout May, though the month marked a pivotal inflection toward de-escalation. Brent crude opened the month above $116/bbl before declining to approximately $110/bbl by mid-to-late May (approx., verify before merging) as diplomatic signals emerged; on May 29, the US and Iran agreed on a preliminary memorandum of understanding extending a ceasefire for 60 days and opening permanent peace negotiations, including unrestricted passage through the Strait of Hormuz. OPEC pledged incremental production increases -- widely characterised as symbolic -- while the UAE's effective departure from the cartel underscored the bloc's diminishing cohesion. Despite oil's retreat from its highs, a geopolitical risk premium of meaningful magnitude persisted, injecting persistent inflationary noise into macro forecasts and complicating central bank communication on the neutral rate path.

Fed Transition -- Warsh Era Begins with a Hold: On May 15, Kevin Warsh was sworn in as the 17th Chair of the Federal Reserve, succeeding Jerome Powell after eight years. In his first FOMC meeting, the Committee voted unanimously to hold the federal funds rate at 3.50%-3.75%, stabilising the contentious 8-4 split that characterised the April meeting. The FOMC revised its core PCE inflation forecast sharply higher to 3.6% for 2026 (from 2.7%), acknowledging that oil-driven cost-push inflation is proving stickier than the look-through framework anticipated. Warsh's rhetoric struck a notably hawkish tone relative to market pricing; futures markets now assign above-30% probability to at least one additional rate hike before year-end, a stark re-pricing from near-zero hike probability at the start of the month. The committee's updated median dot plot implies the bar for cuts has risen materially, forcing a re-rating of duration-sensitive assets and providing tailwinds to the dollar's mid-month recovery.

Record Q1 Earnings Validate the Rally's Foundation: The Q1 2026 earnings season, which concluded in May, delivered the most decisive upside surprise since the post-pandemic recovery cycle. S&P 500 earnings are on pace for approximately 27% year-over-year growth -- more than double the 12% consensus entering the season -- driven by a combination of AI-related revenue acceleration in technology, resilient consumer spending in discretionary, and strong energy-sector cash flows. Megacap names including NVIDIA, Alphabet, and Amazon all reported quarterly results that materially exceeded the highest buy-side estimates, prompting a series of forward guidance upgrades. This earnings momentum provided fundamental underpinning for the price appreciation, distinguishing May's rally from purely sentiment-driven rebounds; the S&P 500's forward P/E multiple expanded only modestly, suggesting the market is broadly paying for genuine earnings power rather than multiple expansion alone.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: The US equity market delivered a historically strong May, with the S&P 500 gaining 5.3% to close at 7,599.96 and the Nasdaq Composite rising 8.9% to 27,086.81 -- the index's best single-month advance since the AI re-rating of 2023. The Dow Jones Industrial Average crossed 51,000 for the first time in its history, settling at 51,078.88. The rally was characterised by extreme concentration in the technology and semiconductor complex: the S&P 500 tech sector accounted for all ten of the index's top individual stock contributors for the month, advancing approximately 20% in aggregate. Breadth, while improving from March's lows, remained skewed toward large-cap growth, with the Russell 2000 modestly underperforming its large-cap peers as higher-for-longer rate expectations weighed on small-cap refinancing costs. Energy's earlier YTD leadership (up 26.8% through late April) was definitively eclipsed by technology's 28.6% total return by month-end, marking a regime change in sector-level return leadership. The nine-week winning streak for the S&P 500 -- observed only four other times in forty years -- reflects an unusual convergence of robust corporate fundamentals, AI-driven sentiment, and easing geopolitical pessimism as May's ceasefire signals began to reduce the headline risk premium.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European equities delivered a volatile but ultimately positive month, with the DAX recovering to approximately 25,105 (approx., verify before merging), representing a gain of approximately 5.0% (approx., verify before merging), while the CAC 40 and FTSE 100 posted more measured recoveries from mid-month weakness. The principal headwinds were the twin pressures of elevated energy costs -- Brent crude remaining above $100/bbl throughout the month -- and renewed political uncertainty in the UK, where Prime Minister Starmer's government faced increasing parliamentary pressure. A sharp mid-month selloff on May 12 and May 18 saw FTSE 100 futures fall approximately 1.7% and continental indices down more than 1%, driven by fears that peace talks with Iran would stall. The subsequent confirmation of a ceasefire extension at month-end provided a meaningful late-month recovery. European corporate earnings were bifurcated: energy-sector names (bp, TotalEnergies, Shell) continued to benefit from elevated hydrocarbon prices, while industrial conglomerates and auto manufacturers flagged margin pressure from elevated input costs. The Stoxx 600 defence sub-index remained a notable outperformer as European NATO members accelerated rearmament spending commitments in response to the Middle East conflict, extending the multi-month trend of defence sector re-rating.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: Asia-Pacific equities posted their strongest monthly performance since the post-COVID recovery, with the MSCI AC Asia Pacific index advancing approximately 7-9% (approx., verify before merging), led by Japanese and broader EM tech names. Japanese equities gained approximately 6.2%, with the Nikkei 225 closing near 66,330 (approx., verify before merging); semiconductor and electronics exporters were the primary contributors as global AI capex demand translated directly into order flow for TSMC supply-chain proxies listed in Tokyo. South Korean and Taiwanese indices similarly outperformed, though both flagged AI chip supply-chain disruptions stemming from the Middle East conflict as an emerging risk -- TSMC, Foxconn, and Infineon each flagged cost and logistical challenges in their earnings calls. The Hang Seng Index closed with a marginal monthly loss of approximately -1.0% (approx., verify before merging), weighed down by ongoing property-sector overhang and a modest geopolitical risk premium on Hong Kong-listed Chinese tech names. The Shanghai Composite advanced approximately 2.7% (approx., verify before merging), supported by resilient domestic consumption data and targeted fiscal stimulus from Beijing's Ministry of Finance. Across the region, the overriding narrative was AI-led re-engagement by global institutional capital, even as management teams cautioned about supply-chain complexity emanating from the Strait of Hormuz closure.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The US Dollar Index (DXY) recovered to approximately 99, posting a gain of roughly 1% for the month (approx., verify before merging), as the hawkish Warsh-era Fed repricing and the dollar's residual safe-haven bid from Middle East uncertainty outweighed the mid-month peace-deal optimism that had briefly pressured the greenback. EUR/USD ended May near 1.165, broadly range-bound as European growth concerns and persistent energy inflation in the Eurozone offset any dollar weakness; the pair's inability to sustain a push above 1.17 reflects the durable headwind of higher US rates for longer. GBP/USD closed near 1.343, pulling back from an early-May push toward the mid-1.36s as UK political uncertainty resurfaced and the Bank of England maintained a cautious tone on rate normalisation given elevated imported inflation. USD/JPY traded in a wide range during the month, settling near 154-156 at month-end (approx., verify before merging); the Bank of Japan's gradual policy normalisation -- with additional rate-hike rhetoric deployed in May -- continued to provide yen support on dips, though the persistent US-Japan rate differential capped the magnitude of any yen appreciation. Commodity-linked currencies (AUD, CAD) tracked oil's declining trajectory through mid-month before recovering on peace-deal signals, ending broadly flat on a DXY-adjusted basis.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: The commodities complex in May was defined by a partial rolling back of the extreme war-risk premium that had characterised March and April, though prices remained structurally elevated relative to pre-conflict levels. Brent crude declined from approximately $116.10/bbl at the month's open to approximately $108-110/bbl at month-end (approx., verify before merging) as Strait of Hormuz tensions eased on ceasefire signals; WTI tracked approximately $3-5/bbl below Brent (approx., verify before merging), consistent with its historical discount, and similarly trended lower through the month. Despite the retreat, both benchmarks remained well above their pre-conflict levels, sustaining a geopolitical risk premium that central banks could not credibly look through indefinitely. Gold consolidated after its January 2026 all-time high of approximately $5,589/oz, trading in the $4,700-4,750/oz range through May (approx., verify before merging) -- a correction of approximately 15% from the peak -- as dollar strength and rising real yields compressed safe-haven demand; J.P. Morgan's end-year target of $6,000/oz nonetheless signals continued institutional conviction in the gold bull case. Copper was the standout outperformer across the metals complex, gaining approximately 6.5% month-over-month to near $6.00/lb, driven by AI data-centre infrastructure demand for electrical components and a structural supply deficit that the market has been pricing since early 2025.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: US Treasuries experienced a bear-steepening dynamic in May as the incoming Warsh FOMC repriced the terminal rate higher while the long end absorbed the dual pressures of fiscal expansion and elevated inflation expectations. The 10-year Treasury yield settled near 4.50-4.56% at month-end (approx., verify before merging), broadly unchanged from mid-month readings near 4.56%, as strong equity risk appetite limited the flight-to-quality bid. The 2-year Treasury yield closed near 4.10-4.13% (approx., verify before merging), reflecting the market's pricing of one potential additional rate hike before year-end and a meaningfully delayed first cut relative to consensus at the start of 2026. The curve remained positively sloped, with the 2s10s spread near +40-45bps -- a normalised configuration following the prolonged inversion of 2023-2024 and a sign of improving growth expectations. The FOMC's upward revision of PCE inflation to 3.6% for 2026 was the central fixed-income story of the month, as it mechanically extended the duration of the higher-for-longer regime and compressed expectations for near-term easing. Credit markets remained well-supported: investment-grade spreads were broadly stable and high-yield spreads tightened modestly as the equity rally and robust earnings bolstered issuer creditworthiness, with new issuance well-absorbed despite the higher rate environment.
